source_text: We ate pasta yesterday evening.

reference_translations.text: Abbiamo mangiato la pasta ieri sera.
reference_translations.notes: Standard rendering. Definite article 'la' with mass-ish food noun is typical in Italian.

reference_translations.text: Ieri sera abbiamo mangiato pasta.
reference_translations.notes: Fronted adverbial; bare 'pasta' (no article) is also acceptable in colloquial Italian.

explanation: Mangiare acts on a direct object (la pasta), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere. 1pl abbiamo. Past participle mangiato. With avere and the direct object following the verb, the past participle stays invariable in -o. Working: abbiamo mangiato la pasta.

source_text: They sold their old car last month.

reference_translations.text: Hanno venduto la loro macchina vecchia il mese scorso.
reference_translations.notes: Standard rendering. Possessive 'la loro' with article.

reference_translations.text: Hanno venduto la loro vecchia macchina il mese scorso.
reference_translations.notes: Adjective fronted before noun: subtle nuance of subjective/inherent quality. Both word orders are fine.

explanation: Vendere is a transitive verb in the regular second-conjugation pattern: -ere → -uto, so venduto. Transitives take avere; 3pl hanno. Working: hanno venduto la macchina.

source_text: I slept for eight hours last night.

reference_translations.text: Ho dormito otto ore ieri notte.
reference_translations.notes: No preposition for duration in Italian (no equivalent of 'for').

reference_translations.text: Ieri notte ho dormito otto ore.
reference_translations.notes: Fronted time adverb is natural and very common.

explanation: Sleeping isn't motion or change, so dormire — though intransitive — takes default avere rather than essere. 1sg ho. Regular third-conjugation past participle dormito. Working: ho dormito otto ore.
examiner_note: A common miss is 'sono dormito' (over-extending essere to all intransitives).

source_text: Marco arrived very late.

reference_translations.text: Marco è arrivato molto tardi.
reference_translations.notes: Aux essere with arrivare; participle agrees masc sg (-o).

explanation: Arrivare is a verb of motion, so it takes essere. With essere, the past participle has to show participle agreement with the subject. Marco is m.sg, so the participle takes -o: arrivato. Working: Marco è arrivato.

source_text: Maria came back from Rome on Sunday.

reference_translations.text: Maria è tornata da Roma domenica.
reference_translations.notes: Aux essere with tornare; participle agrees fem sg (-a). No article with 'domenica' for a specific past day.

reference_translations.text: Maria è tornata da Roma di domenica.
reference_translations.notes: 'di domenica' would mean 'on Sundays' (habitual); avoid unless that meaning is intended.

explanation: Tornare is a verb of motion (returning), so it takes essere. With essere, the past participle has to show participle agreement with the subject. Maria is f.sg, so the participle takes -a: tornata. Working: Maria è tornata.
examiner_note: The classic missed agreement is 'Maria è tornato'.

source_text: The girls left at seven in the morning.

reference_translations.text: Le ragazze sono partite alle sette di mattina.
reference_translations.notes: Aux essere with partire; participle agrees fem pl (-e). 'alle sette di mattina' is the natural collocation.

reference_translations.text: Le ragazze sono partite alle sette del mattino.
reference_translations.notes: 'del mattino' is also acceptable, slightly more formal.

explanation: Partire is a verb of motion, so it takes essere. With essere, the past participle has to show participle agreement with the subject. 'Le ragazze' is f.pl, so the participle takes -e: partite. The natural-feeling miss is the default-masculine -i (partiti); f.pl agreement is the last form most learners get right.

source_text: I got up early this morning.

reference_translations.text: Mi sono alzato presto stamattina.
reference_translations.notes: Masculine speaker. Reflexive alzarsi takes essere; participle agrees masc sg.

reference_translations.text: Mi sono alzata presto stamattina.
reference_translations.notes: Feminine speaker. Without a gender cue in the source, both are valid translations.

reference_translations.text: Stamattina mi sono alzato presto.
reference_translations.notes: Fronted time adverb is natural.

explanation: Alzarsi means getting oneself up, so it's a reflexive verb and takes essere. With essere, the past participle has to agree with the subject's gender. The English source doesn't fix the speaker's gender, so both alzato (m.sg) and alzata (f.sg) are correct. Working: mi sono alzato/a presto.
examiner_note: If the learner declares their gender via the translation-intent metadata, mark accordingly; otherwise accept either form.

source_text: The women had a great time at the party.

reference_translations.text: Le donne si sono divertite molto alla festa.
reference_translations.notes: Reflexive divertirsi: essere. Fem pl subject → -e ending.

reference_translations.text: Le donne si sono divertite tantissimo alla festa.
reference_translations.notes: 'tantissimo' is more colloquial intensifier.

explanation: Divertirsi means enjoying oneself, so it's a reflexive verb and takes essere. With essere, the past participle has to show participle agreement with the subject. 'Le donne' is f.pl, so the participle takes -e: divertite. Working: si sono divertite.

source_text: He did everything by himself.

reference_translations.text: Ha fatto tutto da solo.
reference_translations.notes: Aux avere; irregular participle fatto. 'Da solo' is the standard 'by himself' rendering.

reference_translations.text: Lui ha fatto tutto da solo.
reference_translations.notes: Explicit subject pronoun for emphasis.

explanation: Fare is a transitive verb that takes avere. The past participle is the irregular fatto. 3sg ha; the subject pronoun lui is usually dropped. Working: ha fatto tutto da solo.

source_text: We took the eight o'clock train this morning.

reference_translations.text: Abbiamo preso il treno delle otto stamattina.
reference_translations.notes: Aux avere with prendere; irregular participle preso. 'Delle otto' = of eight.

reference_translations.text: Stamattina abbiamo preso il treno delle otto.
reference_translations.notes: Fronted adverbial; otherwise identical.

explanation: Prendere acts on a direct object (il treno), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere. 1pl abbiamo. The past participle is irregular: preso, not the over-regularised *prenduto. Working: abbiamo preso il treno.

source_text: Yesterday I saw a beautiful film.

reference_translations.text: Ieri ho visto un bel film.
reference_translations.notes: Aux avere; irregular participle visto. 'Bello' becomes 'bel' before consonant-initial masc sg.

reference_translations.text: Ieri ho visto un film bellissimo.
reference_translations.notes: Postposed adjective with superlative — slightly more emphatic.

explanation: Vedere acts on a direct object (un bel film), so it's a transitive verb and takes avere. 1sg ho. The past participle is the irregular visto. Working: ho visto un bel film.

source_text: Did you like the film yesterday?

reference_translations.text: Ti è piaciuto il film ieri?
reference_translations.notes: Piacere takes essere. Grammatical subject is 'il film' (3rd sg masc) so → è piaciuto. Indirect object 'ti'.

reference_translations.text: Il film ti è piaciuto ieri?
reference_translations.notes: Topicalising the subject 'il film' is natural in spoken Italian.

explanation: Italian and English split the 'liking' relationship the opposite way. In Italian, the thing liked is the grammatical subject and the liker is an indirect object pronoun. So 'il film' (m.sg) is the subject and ti is the IOP. Piacere takes essere; the past participle agrees with the subject: piaciuto. Working: ti è piaciuto il film.
examiner_note: Common miss: 'hai piaciuto il film' (Anglophone direct translation).

source_text: I haven't read that book yet.

reference_translations.text: Non ho ancora letto quel libro.
reference_translations.notes: Negation 'non' before auxiliary; adverb 'ancora' between auxiliary and participle.

reference_translations.text: Quel libro non l'ho ancora letto.
reference_translations.notes: Topicalised: 'that book, I haven't read it yet' — natural in spoken Italian, introduces preceding DOP agreement.

explanation: Two rules combine here. Negation placement puts non before the auxiliary. Adverb placement puts ancora between the auxiliary and the past participle. Leggere has the irregular participle letto. Working: non ho ancora letto.

source_text: Did you see Maria's photos? Yes, I saw them yesterday.

reference_translations.text: Hai visto le foto di Maria? Sì, le ho viste ieri.
reference_translations.notes: Second clause has preceding DOP 'le' (fem pl, foto = photographs) so the participle agrees: viste.

reference_translations.text: Hai visto le foto di Maria? Sì, le ho viste ieri pomeriggio.
reference_translations.notes: Slight elaboration; same agreement pattern.

explanation: The same verb behaves differently in the two clauses. First clause: avere with the direct object (le foto) following the verb, so the past participle stays invariable: visto. Second clause: the preceding DOP le forces participle agreement (f.pl, referring to foto), so viste. Same verb, two different agreement outcomes.
examiner_note: The contrast within one item is deliberate: the same verb, two different agreement outcomes.

source_text: Marco read a book and then went to bed.

reference_translations.text: Marco ha letto un libro e poi è andato a letto.
reference_translations.notes: Two-clause sentence: avere with leggere, essere with andare. Aux choice contrast in one sentence.

reference_translations.text: Marco ha letto un libro e dopo è andato a letto.
reference_translations.notes: 'dopo' for 'then' is also fine.

explanation: Marco does two things in one sentence with two different auxiliaries. Leggere is transitive and takes avere: ha letto un libro. Andare is a verb of motion and takes essere: è andato a letto. Same subject, two auxiliaries — a quick test of whether auxiliary choice has really landed.
examiner_note: Anglophones often standardise on 'ha' for both clauses; tracking this distinguishes a true mastery of auxiliary choice.

source_text: Ho parlato con il direttore ieri pomeriggio.

reference_translations.text: I spoke with the director yesterday afternoon.
reference_translations.notes: Standard rendering. PP often maps to simple past in English with a definite past-time adverb.

reference_translations.text: I talked to the director yesterday afternoon.
reference_translations.notes: 'talked to' is equally natural; 'spoke with/to' both work.

reference_translations.text: I have spoken with the director yesterday afternoon.
reference_translations.notes: Grammatically awkward in English: present perfect doesn't take 'yesterday'. Mark as wrong if produced.

explanation: When Italian's passato prossimo carries a definite past-time adverb (ieri pomeriggio), the tense mapping into English picks simple past, not present perfect. So 'I spoke', not 'I have spoken'. Present perfect plus 'yesterday' is ungrammatical in English.
examiner_note: Tests learner sensitivity to the PP-vs-English-tense mapping: PP + definite past adverb → simple past.

source_text: Marco e Anna sono andati al cinema insieme.

reference_translations.text: Marco and Anna went to the cinema together.
reference_translations.notes: Mixed-gender pair → masc pl in Italian; English doesn't mark this.

reference_translations.text: Marco and Anna went to the movies together.
reference_translations.notes: Common AmE variant.

explanation: Andare is a verb of motion and takes essere. Marco (m) and Anna (f) form a mixed-gender plural subject; Italian's mixed-gender default is m.pl agreement on the past participle, so andati. English collapses all of this onto a single 'went'.

source_text: I ragazzi si sono svegliati molto tardi stamattina.

reference_translations.text: The boys woke up very late this morning.
reference_translations.notes: Italian reflexive svegliarsi is rendered as English intransitive 'wake up' (no reflexive pronoun needed).

reference_translations.text: The kids woke up really late this morning.
reference_translations.notes: 'kids' for ragazzi is more informal; 'really' for 'molto' more colloquial.

explanation: Svegliarsi is a reflexive verb (waking oneself up) and takes essere. With essere, the past participle agrees with the subject; 'i ragazzi' is m.pl, so svegliati. English uses a plain intransitive 'wake up' with no reflexive pronoun.
examiner_note: Anglophone learners sometimes literally render 'themselves woke up' — flag it.

source_text: Maria ha scritto una lunga email al suo capo.

reference_translations.text: Maria wrote a long email to her boss.
reference_translations.notes: Standard rendering.

reference_translations.text: Maria has written a long email to her boss.
reference_translations.notes: Acceptable if no definite past-time adverb is present; the source has none, so present perfect is also OK in English.

explanation: Scrivere has the irregular past participle scritto and takes avere. Because the source has no definite past-time adverb, the tense mapping into English allows either simple past ('wrote') or present perfect ('has written'). Both are accepted.

source_text: Lui ha messo le chiavi sul tavolo.

reference_translations.text: He put the keys on the table.
reference_translations.notes: 'Put' is the same form in past tense; simple past.

reference_translations.text: He has put the keys on the table.
reference_translations.notes: Present perfect, no past-time adverb in the source so this also works.

explanation: Mettere is a transitive verb that takes avere. The past participle is the irregular messo. The translation tests recognition of the irregular form. 3sg ha. Working: ha messo le chiavi sul tavolo.

source_text: Mia nonna è nata nel 1940 a Napoli.

reference_translations.text: My grandmother was born in 1940 in Naples.
reference_translations.notes: Nascere ≈ 'to be born' in English, which uses passive 'was born'. Italian uses an active intransitive.

explanation: Nascere is a verb of change of state and takes essere; the past participle is irregular (nato). With essere, the participle agrees with the subject; mia nonna is f.sg, so nata. English realises the same event with a passive ('was born') even though the Italian is active intransitive.
examiner_note: The cross-language structural difference is the test: Italian uses active intransitive where English uses passive.

source_text: Mi sono piaciuti molto i tuoi disegni.

reference_translations.text: I really liked your drawings.
reference_translations.notes: Piacere reverses English subject/object: 'i tuoi disegni' is the Italian subject (masc pl), 'mi' is the indirect object.

reference_translations.text: I liked your drawings a lot.
reference_translations.notes: 'a lot' is a more direct rendering of 'molto'.

explanation: Piacere takes essere; the grammatical subject is the thing liked, here 'i tuoi disegni' (m.pl), so the past participle agrees: piaciuti. The English 'I' is the Italian indirect object pronoun mi. Working: mi sono piaciuti i tuoi disegni.

source_text: Sono corso a casa appena ho sentito la notizia.

reference_translations.text: I ran home as soon as I heard the news.
reference_translations.notes: Combines ambiguous-auxiliary correre (here intransitive motion → essere) and avere with sentire.

reference_translations.text: I ran home the moment I heard the news.
reference_translations.notes: 'The moment' is a stronger 'as soon as'.

explanation: Correre is an ambiguous auxiliary verb. Used here for directional motion (running home), it takes essere: sono corso. The second clause's sentire is transitive and takes avere: ho sentito. Two auxiliaries in one sentence, hinging on what each verb is doing.
examiner_note: Tests recognition that the same verb (correre) can take either auxiliary depending on use.

source_text: Non ho mai visto un film così bello.

reference_translations.text: I have never seen such a beautiful film.
reference_translations.notes: English present perfect because 'never' is durative/up-to-now (no definite past-time adverb).

reference_translations.text: I've never seen such a beautiful movie.
reference_translations.notes: Contraction; 'movie' is AmE.

reference_translations.text: I never saw such a beautiful film.
reference_translations.notes: Simple past is grammatical but implies a past completed period; less natural here than present perfect.

explanation: Three rules combine. Negation placement puts non before the auxiliary. Adverb placement puts mai between auxiliary and past participle. The tense mapping into English picks present perfect because mai is up-to-now. Working: non ho mai visto → have never seen.

source_text: Hai comprato i biglietti? Sì, li ho presi stamattina.

reference_translations.text: Did you buy the tickets? Yes, I got them this morning.
reference_translations.notes: Italian preceding DOP 'li' triggers agreement (presi). English collapses to 'them' with no marking on the verb.

reference_translations.text: Did you get the tickets? Yes, I bought them this morning.
reference_translations.notes: Comprare → buy, prendere → get/pick up; both pairings work as translations.

explanation: Same verb-tense, two agreement outcomes. First clause: avere with the direct object (i biglietti) following, so the participle stays invariable: comprato. Second clause: the preceding DOP li (m.pl, referring to biglietti) forces participle agreement: presi.

source_text: L'ho visto al bar ieri sera.

reference_translations.text: I saw him at the bar last night.
reference_translations.notes: L' here = lo (masc sg), referring to a male person. Participle agreement is -o (invisible: same as default).

reference_translations.text: I saw it at the bar last night.
reference_translations.notes: L' could also refer to an inanimate masc sg referent, depending on context.

explanation: Here l' is the elided lo (m.sg DOP). When a DOP precedes the verb in the passato prossimo, the past participle agrees with it: m.sg → -o, so visto. The agreement is invisible here because the default ending is also -o, but the rule is still being applied.
examiner_note: Useful contrast with the la-form question (l'ho vista) where the -a agreement is visible.

source_text: Mia sorella è arrivata tardi e ha mangiato in cucina.

reference_translations.text: My sister arrived late and ate in the kitchen.
reference_translations.notes: Two-clause sentence: essere with arrivare, avere with mangiare. Same subject, two auxiliaries.

reference_translations.text: My sister got home late and ate in the kitchen.
reference_translations.notes: 'Got home' is a slightly looser rendering.

explanation: One subject, two verbs, two different patterns. Arrivare is motion and takes essere; with essere the participle agrees with mia sorella (f.sg), so arrivata. Mangiare is transitive and takes avere; with no preceding DOP the participle stays invariable: mangiato.
examiner_note: Lots of contrastive skills in one sentence: auxiliary choice both ways and agreement on essere but not avere.

source_text: I bambini hanno bevuto troppa cioccolata.

reference_translations.text: The children drank too much hot chocolate.
reference_translations.notes: 'Cioccolata' as a drink = hot chocolate; as a substance = chocolate.

reference_translations.text: The kids drank too much hot chocolate.
reference_translations.notes: 'Kids' is more informal.

explanation: Bere is a transitive verb that takes avere. The past participle bevuto shows stem expansion: bere comes from older bevere, and the older stem reappears in the participle. 3pl hanno. Working: hanno bevuto troppa cioccolata.

source_text: Marco ha aperto la finestra e poi l'ha chiusa di nuovo.

reference_translations.text: Marco opened the window and then closed it again.
reference_translations.notes: Second clause has preceding DOP l' = la (fem sg, la finestra) so chiudere → chiusa with -a agreement.

reference_translations.text: Marco opened the window and then shut it again.
reference_translations.notes: 'shut' is a fine variant of 'closed'.

explanation: Two irregular past participles (aperto, chiuso) plus a contrast in the same sentence. First clause: avere with following DO (la finestra), so the participle stays invariable: aperto. Second clause: the preceding DOP l' (= la, f.sg) forces participle agreement: chiusa.
examiner_note: Dense skill load in one short sentence; useful diagnostic.

source_text: Sono stato a Roma per una settimana l'anno scorso.

reference_translations.text: I was in Rome for a week last year.
reference_translations.notes: Masculine speaker (implied by 'stato'). English uses 'was' for the stative meaning.

reference_translations.text: I spent a week in Rome last year.
reference_translations.notes: More idiomatic for the same situation; loses the literal 'be'.

reference_translations.text: I have been to Rome for a week last year.
reference_translations.notes: Grammatically odd in English (present perfect with definite past-time adverb). Mark as wrong if produced.

explanation: In compound tenses, essere takes itself as its auxiliary, and the past participle is the irregular stato (shared with stare). The participle agrees with the subject; m.sg speaker → stato. The tense mapping into English picks simple past 'was' because 'l'anno scorso' is a definite past-time adverb. Working: sono stato a Roma.
examiner_note: Tests the essere/stare shared-participle bucket plus the PP↔English-tense mapping rule.

source_text: I have already eaten.

reference_translations.text: Ho già mangiato.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Adverb 'già' between auxiliary and participle.

reference_translations.text: Già ho mangiato.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: pre-positioned adverb. Anglophone interference pattern.

reference_translations.text: Ho mangiato già.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: post-positioned adverb. Anglophone interference pattern.

explanation: Italian's adverb placement rule puts già between the auxiliary and the past participle: ho già mangiato. The negative anchors flag the two common Anglophone misses, pre-posing ('già ho mangiato') and post-posing ('ho mangiato già').

source_text: Sono appena tornati a casa.

reference_translations.text: They have just come home.
reference_translations.notes: Present perfect because the adverb is up-to-now ('just'). Subject masc pl from 'tornati'.

reference_translations.text: They've just gotten home.
reference_translations.notes: AmE colloquial.

reference_translations.text: They just came home.
reference_translations.notes: Simple past is acceptable in AmE conversational use; less natural in BrE.

translation.reveal: tornare - to return / to come back

explanation: Italian's adverb placement rule puts appena between the auxiliary and the past participle: sono appena tornati. The tense mapping into English picks present perfect because appena is an up-to-now adverb: 'have just come home'.

source_text: Ieri ho visto un bel film al cinema.

reference_translations.text: Yesterday I saw a beautiful film at the cinema.
reference_translations.notes: Simple past required because 'ieri' is a definite past-time adverb.

reference_translations.text: I saw a beautiful film at the cinema yesterday.
reference_translations.notes: Adverb in final position; otherwise identical.

reference_translations.text: Yesterday I have seen a beautiful film at the cinema.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: English present perfect is ungrammatical with a definite past-time adverb. Common Anglophone-learner-of-Italian-then-translating-back miss.

explanation: When Italian's passato prossimo carries a definite past-time adverb like ieri, the tense mapping into English picks simple past. So 'I saw', not 'I have seen'. English present perfect with 'yesterday' is ungrammatical, which is why the negative anchor is included.

source_text: Due settimane fa Maria è andata a Roma.

reference_translations.text: Two weeks ago Maria went to Rome.
reference_translations.notes: Simple past. 'Two weeks ago' fixes the time.

reference_translations.text: Maria went to Rome two weeks ago.
reference_translations.notes: Adverbial in final position.

reference_translations.text: Two weeks ago Maria has gone to Rome.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: present perfect with definite past-time adverb.

explanation: 'Due settimane fa' (= two weeks ago) is a definite past-time adverbial, so the tense mapping into English picks simple past: 'went'. Italian side: andare is a verb of motion and takes essere, with the past participle agreeing with f.sg Maria as andata.

source_text: L'anno scorso mio fratello ha cambiato lavoro.

reference_translations.text: Last year my brother changed jobs.
reference_translations.notes: Simple past. 'Last year' fixes the time.

reference_translations.text: My brother changed jobs last year.
reference_translations.notes: Adverbial in final position.

reference_translations.text: Last year my brother has changed jobs.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: present perfect with 'last year'.

explanation: 'L'anno scorso' (= last year) is a definite past-time adverbial, so the tense mapping into English picks simple past: 'changed', not 'has changed'. Italian side: cambiare here is transitive (taking lavoro as object) and takes avere; regular -ato participle.

source_text: Stamattina alle otto abbiamo preso il treno.

reference_translations.text: This morning at eight we took the train.
reference_translations.notes: Simple past. 'Stamattina' is conventionally a definite past adverbial in narration.

reference_translations.text: We took the eight o'clock train this morning.
reference_translations.notes: Slight idiomatic shift but same tense logic.

reference_translations.text: This morning at eight we have taken the train.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: present perfect with definite past-time adverb.

explanation: 'Stamattina alle otto' anchors a specific moment in the past, so the tense mapping into English picks simple past: 'we took', not 'we have taken'. Italian side: prendere is transitive and takes avere, with the irregular past participle preso.

source_text: Ho già fatto i compiti.

reference_translations.text: I've already done my homework.
reference_translations.notes: Present perfect. 'Già' (= already) is an up-to-now adverb that triggers present perfect in English.

reference_translations.text: I have already done my homework.
reference_translations.notes: Uncontracted; same meaning.

reference_translations.text: I already did my homework yesterday.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: only correct if 'yesterday' is present in the source; here it isn't. Simple past would need a past-time anchor.

explanation: Già is an up-to-now adverb, so the tense mapping into English picks present perfect: 'have already done'. Italian side: adverb placement puts già between the auxiliary and the past participle (ho già fatto), and fare's past participle is the irregular fatto.

source_text: Non hai ancora finito il libro?

reference_translations.text: Haven't you finished the book yet?
reference_translations.notes: Present perfect, inverted for the question. 'Ancora' in a negative = 'yet'.

reference_translations.text: You haven't finished the book yet?
reference_translations.notes: Declarative-with-rising-intonation question; natural in spoken English.

explanation: Non...ancora (= not yet) is the classic up-to-now construction, so the tense mapping into English picks present perfect: 'haven't finished yet'. Italian's adverb placement puts ancora between the auxiliary and the past participle; English moves 'yet' to the end of the sentence.

source_text: Non ho mai mangiato il sushi.

reference_translations.text: I've never eaten sushi.
reference_translations.notes: Present perfect because 'mai' (= never) is durative/up-to-now. English drops the definite article on category nouns.

reference_translations.text: I have never eaten sushi.
reference_translations.notes: Uncontracted.

reference_translations.text: I never ate sushi.
reference_translations.notes: Simple past acceptable when implying a closed period (e.g. 'as a child'); less natural for an open-ended life statement.

explanation: Mai in non...mai (= never) is an up-to-now adverb, so the tense mapping into English picks present perfect: 'I've never eaten'. Italian uses a definite article on category nouns (il sushi); English drops it.

source_text: Mia sorella è appena arrivata.

reference_translations.text: My sister has just arrived.
reference_translations.notes: Present perfect because 'appena' (= just) is up-to-now.

reference_translations.text: My sister's just arrived.
reference_translations.notes: Contracted, conversational.

reference_translations.text: My sister just arrived.
reference_translations.notes: Simple past is widely acceptable in AmE for very-recent past; BrE prefers present perfect.

explanation: Appena (= just) is an up-to-now adverb, so the tense mapping into English picks present perfect: 'has just arrived'. Italian side: arrivare is a verb of motion and takes essere; the participle agrees with f.sg sister, so arrivata.

source_text: Have you read these books? Yes, I've read three of them.

reference_translations.text: Hai letto questi libri? Sì, ne ho letti tre.
reference_translations.notes: Standard rendering. Second clause uses partitive 'ne' with participle agreement: letti (masc pl, agreeing with libri).

reference_translations.text: Hai letto questi libri? Sì, ne ho letto tre.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: missing agreement. Common miss when learners forget that 'ne' triggers participle agreement.

explanation: English 'of them' maps to Italian's partitive ne. When ne precedes the verb in the passato prossimo, the past participle agrees with the implicit referent: libri is m.pl, so letti. The negative anchor flags the no-agreement miss (letto), which is the most common error on this rule.
examiner_note: Negative anchor flags the no-agreement miss explicitly. This is the most common error on this skill.

source_text: Anna and her two brothers went to the cinema together.

reference_translations.text: Anna e i suoi due fratelli sono andati al cinema insieme.
reference_translations.notes: Mixed-gender plural subject → masc pl participle (andati).

reference_translations.text: Anna e i suoi due fratelli sono andate al cinema insieme.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: fem pl agreement applied to a mixed-gender group. Common modern-language-aware miss.

explanation: Anna (f) + due fratelli (m.pl) is a mixed-gender plural subject. Italian doesn't count proportions: any masculine in the group triggers the mixed-gender default, so the past participle takes m.pl agreement: andati. Andare is motion and takes essere.

source_text: Yesterday Maria had to leave early.

reference_translations.text: Ieri Maria è dovuta partire presto.
reference_translations.notes: Prescriptive: dovere inherits essere from partire. Participle dovuta agrees with fem sg subject.

reference_translations.text: Ieri Maria ha dovuto partire presto.
reference_translations.notes: Colloquial: dovere uses its own auxiliary (avere). Widely accepted in spoken Italian; less so in written/formal.

reference_translations.text: Ieri Maria è dovuto partire presto.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: essere is right (prescriptive) but the participle fails to agree with the feminine singular subject.

explanation: Dovere is a modal verb here governing partire. Partire is a verb of motion and takes essere, so prescriptive Italian shows modal auxiliary inheritance: dovere takes essere too. The participle agrees with f.sg Maria: è dovuta partire. Colloquial Italian also accepts ha dovuto partire.
examiner_note: Score the prescriptive form at full credit and the colloquial form at full credit too (both are current Italian); the negative anchor catches the no-agreement miss.

source_text: Siamo potuti partire in orario solo grazie a te.

reference_translations.text: We were able to leave on time only thanks to you.
reference_translations.notes: Simple past. 'In orario' anchors the moment.

reference_translations.text: We could only leave on time thanks to you.
reference_translations.notes: More idiomatic 'could' for ability in the past, with 'only' modifying the cause.

translation.reveal: potere - to be able to / can
orario - timetable / schedule (in orario = on time)

explanation: Potere is a modal verb governing partire. Partire is a verb of motion, so by modal auxiliary inheritance potere takes essere too: siamo potuti partire. The participle is in m.pl agreement with the implied noi. English renders modality + past as 'were able to' or 'could'.

source_text: Last week the girls bought the tickets.

reference_translations.text: La settimana scorsa le ragazze hanno comprato i biglietti.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Avere with comprare; 3rd plural → hanno; participle invariable (no preceding DOP).

reference_translations.text: Le ragazze hanno comprato i biglietti la settimana scorsa.
reference_translations.notes: Adverbial in final position.

reference_translations.text: La settimana scorsa le ragazze ha comprato i biglietti.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: right family (avere) but wrong person (singular ha for plural subject). Common person-agreement miss.

explanation: Comprare is a transitive verb that takes avere. 'Le ragazze' is 3pl, so person agreement gives hanno: hanno comprato. The negative anchor flags the right-family-wrong-person miss (singular ha for plural subject), which is the diagnostic the person agreement bucket was created to catch.

source_text: Did you and Maria see the film yesterday evening?

reference_translations.text: Tu e Maria avete visto il film ieri sera?
reference_translations.notes: Standard. 'Tu e X' collapses to voi (2nd plural) → avete.

reference_translations.text: Ieri sera tu e Maria avete visto il film?
reference_translations.notes: Adverbial fronted.

reference_translations.text: Tu e Maria hanno visto il film ieri sera?
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: defaulting to 3rd-plural hanno because Maria is 3rd. The 'tu e X → voi' collapse is the diagnostic.

explanation: When Italian combines tu with another person ('tu e X'), the resulting subject becomes voi (2pl) — that's the tu-X collapse. Vedere takes avere, so person agreement gives avete, not hanno. The diagnostic miss is defaulting to 3rd person because of Maria.

source_text: Yesterday Marco told the truth to his parents.

reference_translations.text: Ieri Marco ha detto la verità ai suoi genitori.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Avere with dire; irregular participle detto.

reference_translations.text: Marco ieri ha detto la verità ai suoi genitori.
reference_translations.notes: Adverb shift; otherwise identical.

explanation: Dire is a transitive verb that takes avere; the past participle is the irregular detto (a stem expansion verb, from older dicere). Tense mapping: ieri is a definite past-time adverb, so English picks simple past 'told'. Working: ha detto la verità.

source_text: Il loro gatto è morto la settimana scorsa.

reference_translations.text: Their cat died last week.
reference_translations.notes: Simple past (definite past-time adverb).

reference_translations.text: Their cat passed away last week.
reference_translations.notes: Euphemistic register; same tense logic.

reference_translations.text: Their cat has died last week.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: present perfect with 'last week' is ungrammatical in English.

explanation: Morire is a verb of change of state and takes essere; the past participle is the irregular morto, agreeing with m.sg gatto. Tense mapping: 'la settimana scorsa' is a definite past-time adverb, so English picks simple past 'died' (or the more euphemistic 'passed away').

source_text: Anna's friends came to the party with their parents.

reference_translations.text: Gli amici di Anna sono venuti alla festa con i loro genitori.
reference_translations.notes: Standard. Essere with venire; participle venuti agrees with masc pl 'amici'.

reference_translations.text: Gli amici di Anna sono venuti alla festa insieme ai loro genitori.
reference_translations.notes: 'Insieme ai' slightly more emphatic on accompaniment.

reference_translations.text: Gli amici di Anna hanno venuto alla festa con i loro genitori.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: avere with venire is the default-avere over-application. Venire takes essere.

explanation: Venire is a verb of motion and takes essere. The past participle is irregular: venuto (it looks like a regular -uto but the stem changes from veni- to venu-). 'Gli amici' is m.pl, so the participle agrees: venuti. Working: sono venuti alla festa.

source_text: Did you write the letter? Yes, I wrote it this morning.

reference_translations.text: Hai scritto la lettera? Sì, l'ho scritta stamattina.
reference_translations.notes: Second clause has preceding DOP l' (= la, fem sg, referring to 'la lettera') so the participle agrees: scritta.

reference_translations.text: L'hai scritta la lettera? Sì, l'ho scritta stamattina.
reference_translations.notes: Topicalised first clause (clitic doubling); the agreement pattern is the same.

reference_translations.text: Hai scritto la lettera? Sì, l'ho scritto stamattina.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: no agreement on the participle in the second clause. The preceding DOP l' (= la) requires fem sg agreement.

explanation: Same verb, two agreement outcomes side by side. First clause: avere with the direct object (la lettera) following the verb, so the past participle stays invariable: scritto. Second clause: the preceding DOP l' (= la, f.sg) forces participle agreement: scritta. Negative anchor catches the no-agreement miss.
examiner_note: The contrast is the diagnostic: a learner who writes 'l'ho scritto' has missed the preceding-DOP rule even though they got the first clause right.
